title: Waxing and buffing bare wood
subtitle: Apply a seal, wax, and buff bare or stripped timber to a soft, durable finish
excerpt: How to seal and wax bare or stripped wood: applying a shellac sanding sealer to close the grain, sanding back between coats, applying beeswax or carnauba wax, and buffing to a soft sheen that protects the timber without a hard plastic surface layer.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: A
shellac sanding sealer
applied before waxing is what gives a
wax finish
its depth and consistency. Wax applied directly to unsealed bare timber raises the grain, soaks into porous areas unevenly, and produces a dull, patchy result that cannot be buffed to a sheen.
Step 1, sand the bare wood to final grit

Sand the bare wood through progressive grits: 120-grit to remove any remaining old finish or scratches, 180-grit to refine the surface, and 240-grit for the final pass before sealing. Sand always with the grain; cross-grain scratches from a coarser grit show through a wax finish under raking light. After the final sand, wipe the surface with a lint-free cloth damped with methylated spirit to raise any remaining nibs and remove all dust.
Step 2, prepare and apply the shellac sealer

Dissolve blonde or dewaxed shellac flakes in methylated spirit at a ratio of one part flakes by weight to eight parts methylated spirit (a one-pound cut). Stir until fully dissolved; this takes 20 to 30 minutes. Apply one thin coat with a lint-free cloth or a french polish rubber in long, even strokes with the grain. The first coat raises the grain slightly. Allow to dry for 20 minutes, then sand very lightly with 320-grit to knock back the raised fibres. Apply a second coat and allow to dry.
Step 3, apply the wax

Apply a thin coat of beeswax or furniture wax with a soft cloth, working it into the grain in circular motions. A thin coat applied in a fine layer outperforms a thick coat; a thick application cannot be polished and dries to a greasy, dulled surface. Allow the wax to haze, typically 5 to 10 minutes depending on temperature. The surface should have a flat, slightly clouded appearance when ready for buffing.
Step 4, buff to a sheen

Buff the surface vigorously with a clean, dry cloth using long strokes with the grain. The friction builds warmth in the wax and brings up the sheen. A second coat applied after the first is fully buffed produces a deeper, more even result. A wax finish does not harden to a protective shell; it sits in the surface fibres and is renewed by additional wax when it becomes dull. Re-wax every 6 to 12 months on a working surface.
Common mistakes
Waxing over an unsealed surface.
Wax on raw, unsealed wood soaks into the grain unevenly and cannot be buffed. The shellac sealer is the essential first step.
Applying too thick a wax coat.
A heavy application takes too long to haze and smears rather than polishes. Use less wax than seems necessary: the second coat builds the finish, not the first.
Using dewaxed shellac without re-waxing on top.
Dewaxed shellac is a sealer, not a finish. A raw shellac sealer left without wax is dry, chalky, and unprotected. Always apply wax over the sealer.
